new_or_modified_concept: National IHR Authority
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces a new definition: 'National IHR Authority means the entity designated or established by the State Party at the national level to coordinate the implementation of these Regulations within the jurisdiction of the State Party.' This is a brand-new legal concept absent from IHR 2005.
notes: This definition provides the legal basis for Art. 4(1) and Art. 4(1 bis). Every State Party must now have both a National IHR Authority and a National IHR Focal Point. These may or may not be the same entity. Requires review of domestic institutional architecture to determine whether a new entity must be created or an existing one formally designated. Uncertainty: the definition does not prescribe the legal form (ministry, agency, inter-ministerial body) or required powers of the National IHR Authority.

new_or_modified_concept: pandemic emergency
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces a new definition: 'pandemic emergency means a public health emergency of international concern that is caused by a communicable disease and meets four cumulative criteria: (i) wide geographical spread to multiple States or high risk thereof; (ii) exceeding or at high risk of exceeding health system capacity in those States; (iii) causing substantial social and/or economic disruption; (iv) requiring rapid, equitable and enhanced coordinated international action with whole-of-government and whole-of-society approaches.'
notes: A pandemic emergency is a sub-category of PHEIC, not a separate legal category. It triggers additional DG determination obligations under Art. 12(4 bis) and activates Art. 13(8) equitable access provisions. The 'whole-of-government and whole-of-society' criterion implies that domestic emergency response frameworks may need to explicitly address pandemic emergencies and not solely general PHEICs. Legal review required to determine whether existing domestic public health emergency legislation distinguishes or needs to distinguish pandemic emergencies.

new_or_modified_concept: relevant health products
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces a new definition: 'relevant health products means those health products needed to respond to public health emergencies of international concern, including pandemic emergencies, which may include medicines, vaccines, diagnostics, medical devices, vector control products, personal protective equipment, decontamination products, assistive products, antidotes, cell- and gene-based therapies, and other health technologies.'
notes: This definition operationalizes the equitable access obligations in Arts. 13(8), 15(2), 16(1), 17(d bis), and 18(3). It is deliberately broad and non-exhaustive ('may include'). The domestic relevance depends on how national public health emergency legislation defines the scope of health products subject to regulatory fast-track, stockpiling obligations, or price regulation during emergencies. Legal review recommended to assess regulatory coverage of all listed categories, in particular cell- and gene-based therapies and assistive products.

new_or_modified_concept: preparedness added to IHR purpose
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 2 to read: 'to prevent, prepare for, protect against, control and provide a public health response to the international spread of disease.' The phrase 'prepare for' is new; IHR 2005 read 'to prevent, protect against, control.'
notes: Preparedness is now an express part of the IHR's purpose, not merely an implicit element of core capacity requirements. This may affect how domestic courts and administrative bodies interpret State obligations under the IHR and may provide additional grounds for challenging inadequate preparedness planning. Domestic legislation that limits IHR implementation to detection and response (without explicit preparedness mandate) may require amendment. Cross-references Annex 1 expanded title and Art. 13(1).

new_or_modified_concept: equity and solidarity added to implementation principles
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 3(1) to read: 'The implementation of these Regulations shall be with full respect for the dignity, human rights and fundamental freedoms of persons, and shall promote equity and solidarity.' The phrase 'and shall promote equity and solidarity' is new.
notes: Equity and solidarity are new normative principles binding implementation. This may influence judicial interpretation of IHR-based domestic health measures. 'Equity' has particular relevance for access to health products under Art. 13(8) and Art. 17(d bis). 'Solidarity' implies cooperative duties beyond strict legal obligations. Legal review recommended to assess whether domestic public health legislation reflects these principles and whether they affect administrative discretion in implementing IHR obligations.

new_or_modified_concept: National IHR Authority designation or establishment
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 4(1) to require each State Party to designate or establish 'one or two entities to serve as National IHR Authority and a National IHR Focal Point.' The IHR 2005 required only designation of a National IHR Focal Point. The National IHR Authority is an entirely new institutional requirement.
notes: States may designate one entity to serve dual functions or two separate entities. The phrase 'in accordance with its national law and context' provides flexibility but does not remove the obligation. Requires formal legal/regulatory act designating the National IHR Authority. If no existing entity is sufficient, a new one must be established. This is among the most significant institutional changes introduced by WHA77.17. Cross-references Art. 4(1 bis) (coordination function) and Art. 4(2 bis) (domestic legislative adjustments).

new_or_modified_concept: National IHR Authority coordination function
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 4(1 bis): 'The National IHR Authority shall coordinate the implementation of these Regulations within the jurisdiction of the State Party.' This is a new mandatory coordination function with no IHR 2005 equivalent.
notes: The coordination function is assigned to the National IHR Authority, not the National IHR Focal Point (which retains its communications function). These are legally distinct responsibilities. In federal systems, 'within the jurisdiction of the State Party' must encompass subnational levels, raising questions of inter-governmental coordination authority. The coordination mandate may require legislative definition of which sectors and levels of government the National IHR Authority can direct.

new_or_modified_concept: domestic legislative and administrative adjustments
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 4(2 bis): 'States Parties shall take measures to implement paragraphs 1, 1 bis, and 2 of this Article, including, as appropriate, adjusting their domestic legislative and/or administrative arrangements.' This is the clearest explicit domestic implementation trigger in the 2024 amendments.
notes: Art. 4(2 bis) is unusual in explicitly requiring domestic legislative and administrative adjustment. The phrase 'as appropriate' introduces some flexibility but does not eliminate the general obligation to take implementing measures. This provision effectively mandates a domestic legal review of existing authorities and, where gaps exist, enactment of new legislation or administrative regulations. For Mexico, this requires assessing whether the CENAPRECE, Secretaría de Salud, or a new entity can serve as National IHR Authority with sufficient legal mandate.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO notification of National IHR Authority contact details
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 4(4) to require States Parties to provide WHO with contact details of 'their National IHR Authority and their National IHR Focal Point' (added National IHR Authority). IHR 2005 required only NFP contact details.
notes: Administrative extension of the existing NFP contact-detail notification obligation. Requires a designated authority to maintain and annually confirm both sets of contact details. Where the National IHR Authority and NFP are the same entity, this may be administratively simple; where they differ, both must be kept current. Cross-references Art. 4(1) designation requirement.

new_or_modified_concept: core capacities expanded to include prevention
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 5(1) to require 'core capacities to prevent, detect, assess, notify and report events.' IHR 2005 required capacity 'to detect, assess, notify and report events.' The word 'prevent' is new.
notes: Prevention as a core capacity obligation is a significant normative expansion. It aligns with the expanded Annex 1 which now covers 'prevention, surveillance, preparedness and response.' This may require domestic public health legislation to include prevention-specific mandates within the IHR framework, beyond surveillance and response. Cross-references Annex 1 (IHR24-CHG-051 to IHR24-CHG-054). Art. 5(3) also now refers to 'core capacities' (minor language alignment).

new_or_modified_concept: notification to intergovernmental organizations beyond IAEA
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 6(1) to require WHO to notify 'the IAEA or, as appropriate, the other competent intergovernmental organization(s)' when notification involves their competency. IHR 2005 required notification only to IAEA.
notes: This change affects WHO's notification procedure, not State Party obligations directly. No new domestic anchoring required. However, States Parties should be aware that notifications involving chemical, biological, or other events may now trigger WHO notifications to organizations beyond IAEA (e.g., OPCW, FAO/IPPC). This may affect how States Parties categorize events in their Annex 2 assessments.

new_or_modified_concept: consultation obligation strengthened from 'may' to 'should'
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 8 to read: 'a State Party should nevertheless keep WHO advised.' IHR 2005 used 'may.' Additionally, the phrase 'in a timely manner' is added at the end of the consultation sentence.
notes: The change from 'may' to 'should' and the addition of 'in a timely manner' strengthens the normative expectation for sub-threshold event consultation, though it remains short of a binding obligation. This reflects a shift toward a stronger duty of proactive information sharing. 'Should' in IHR context is generally understood as a strong recommendation rather than a strict obligation. Domestic procedures for sub-threshold event assessment and NFP/National IHR Authority consultation should be reviewed.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO collaboration offer triggered earlier; WHO 'should' share information
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 10(3) to provide that 'Upon receiving information of an event' (replacing 'When WHO receives'), WHO shall offer collaboration. Art. 10(4) changes 'may share' to 'should share' information if State Party does not accept collaboration. These are WHO-facing obligations.
notes: These changes primarily bind WHO, not States Parties. The earlier triggering of WHO's collaboration offer (Art. 10(3)) and the strengthened expectation to share information (Art. 10(4)) may increase pressure on States Parties to accept collaboration rather than face WHO sharing event information. No new domestic legal anchoring required, but States Parties should update their internal protocols for responding to WHO collaboration offers.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO information sharing triggered by pandemic emergency
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 11(2)(a) to provide that WHO may make information generally available to other States Parties when 'the event is determined to constitute a public health emergency of international concern, including a pandemic emergency.' The phrase 'including a pandemic emergency' is new.
notes: A WHO-facing change. The trigger for broader information sharing now explicitly includes pandemic emergency determinations. No new domestic anchoring required; however, this highlights that States Parties should ensure their information-sharing protocols account for the possibility that event information will be shared more broadly upon a pandemic emergency determination. Links to Art. 12(4 bis) (two-step PHEIC/pandemic emergency determination).

new_or_modified_concept: pandemic emergency as a sub-determination of PHEIC
change_summary: WHA77.17 significantly amends Art. 12: the title now reads 'Determination of a public health emergency of international concern, including a pandemic emergency.' Art. 12(1) empowers the DG to determine 'including, when appropriate, a pandemic emergency.' Art. 12(4 bis) (new) requires the DG, upon determining a PHEIC, to further determine whether it also constitutes a pandemic emergency. Art. 12(5) adds that a PHEIC/pandemic emergency ends when it no longer meets 'the relevant definition in Article 1.'
notes: A two-step determination procedure is introduced: (1) PHEIC determination; (2) if PHEIC, further pandemic emergency determination. Both are Director-General decisions following Emergency Committee consultation. The pandemic emergency determination activates the Art. 13(8) equitable access obligations and potentially more intensive WHO coordination under Art. 13(7). States Parties must ensure domestic emergency response plans are calibrated to both PHEIC and pandemic emergency triggers. The 'whole-of-government and whole-of-society' criteria in the pandemic emergency definition imply broader domestic coordination than a standard PHEIC.

new_or_modified_concept: multi-State Party consultation for PHEIC determination
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Arts. 12(2) and 12(3) to refer to 'State(s) Party(ies)' (plural) in whose territory the event 'is occurring' (replacing 'arises'). This explicitly recognizes multi-State PHEIC events and updates terminology to reflect ongoing rather than point-in-time events.
notes: A minor but normatively relevant clarification: when a PHEIC involves multiple States, all affected States are formally consulted. 'Arises' replaced by 'is occurring' aligns the text with the evolving nature of public health emergencies. No new domestic anchoring required. Useful clarification for States' understanding of their consultative role in PHEIC determinations.

new_or_modified_concept: title expanded to include equitable access to relevant health products
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends the title of Art. 13 to 'Public health response, including equitable access to relevant health products.' This is a significant normative signal that equitable access is now embedded within the core response obligations framework.
notes: The title change reflects the introduction of Arts. 13(7)-(9) which create substantive equitable access obligations. States Parties must now consider equitable access as part of their public health response planning. Domestic emergency legislation and procurement frameworks should be reviewed for compatibility with the equitable access principle.

new_or_modified_concept: core capacities expanded to include prevention, preparedness, humanitarian settings
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 13(1) to require 'core capacities to prevent, prepare for, and respond promptly and effectively to public health risks and public health emergencies of international concern, including a pandemic emergency, including in fragile and humanitarian settings.' IHR 2005 required only capacity 'to respond promptly and effectively.'
notes: Prevention and preparedness are now explicit response core capacity obligations alongside response. 'Including in fragile and humanitarian settings' is new and requires States Parties to plan for contexts where standard governance structures may not function. This implies domestic emergency preparedness plans must address fragile settings and humanitarian situations, which may require inter-ministerial coordination (health, civil protection, foreign affairs). Cross-references Annex 1 expanded capacities.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO coordination of international response during PHEIC and pandemic emergency
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 13(7): 'WHO shall support States Parties, upon their request or following acceptance of an offer from WHO, and coordinate international response activities during public health emergencies of international concern, including pandemic emergencies, after their determination pursuant to Article 12 of these Regulations.' This is a new explicit WHO coordination mandate.
notes: This is primarily a WHO obligation. However, it implies that States Parties should have clear protocols for requesting WHO coordination support and for accepting such offers. Domestic procedures for engaging with WHO during PHEICs and pandemic emergencies should account for this new coordination mandate.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO obligation to facilitate equitable access to relevant health products
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 13(8) with five sub-paragraphs (a)-(e): WHO shall facilitate timely and equitable access to relevant health products after PHEIC/pandemic emergency determination. WHO obligations include: needs assessments and their publication; use of WHO-coordinated mechanisms; supporting scale-up and geographic diversification of production; sharing product dossiers on request within 30 days; supporting research and development.
notes: Primarily a WHO obligation, but Art. 13(8)(b) implies States Parties should engage with WHO-coordinated access mechanisms. Art. 13(8)(c) on geographic diversification of production may create expectations for domestic manufacturing capacity development. Art. 13(8)(d) on product dossier sharing requires domestic regulatory capacity to evaluate shared dossiers. Art. 13(9) creates corresponding State Party obligations to collaborate. Legal review recommended to assess regulatory readiness for emergency use authorization, stockpiling, and production scale-up.

new_or_modified_concept: State Party collaboration for equitable access and WHO-coordinated response
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 13(9): States Parties shall, subject to applicable law and available resources, collaborate with each other and support WHO-coordinated response activities, including by: (a) supporting WHO Art. 13(8) actions; (b) engaging relevant stakeholders to facilitate equitable access to relevant health products; (c) making available terms of research and development agreements related to relevant health products.
notes: Art. 13(9)(c) is significant: States Parties shall make available 'relevant terms of their research and development agreements for relevant health products related to promoting equitable access.' This may require review of confidentiality clauses in existing R&D and procurement agreements with manufacturers. Legal review of domestic R&D agreement frameworks recommended. The qualifications 'subject to applicable law and available resources' provide some flexibility but do not remove the obligation.

new_or_modified_concept: temporary recommendations scope includes pandemic emergency and relevant health products
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Arts. 15(1) and 15(3) to include 'pandemic emergency' alongside PHEIC throughout. Art. 15(2) adds 'including relevant health products' after 'goods.' Art. 15(2 bis) (new) requires DG to provide information on WHO-coordinated mechanisms for access to relevant health products when communicating temporary recommendations.
notes: Temporary recommendations now explicitly encompass relevant health products (within the goods category) and must be accompanied by information on access mechanisms. States Parties must be operationally ready to implement recommendations concerning relevant health products. Domestic regulatory frameworks for implementing WHO recommendations on health product access, allocation, and distribution should be reviewed.

new_or_modified_concept: standing recommendations scope includes relevant health products and access information
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 16(1) to add 'including relevant health products' after goods in standing recommendations. Art. 16(2) (new) requires DG to provide information on WHO-coordinated mechanisms for access to relevant health products when communicating standing recommendations.
notes: Parallel to Art. 15 changes. Standing recommendations now also encompass relevant health products. Domestic frameworks for implementing WHO standing recommendations should be reviewed to ensure they cover the full range of relevant health products as defined in Art. 1.

new_or_modified_concept: availability and accessibility of relevant health products as new criterion for recommendations
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 17(d bis): When issuing, modifying or terminating temporary or standing recommendations, the DG shall consider 'availability of, and accessibility to relevant health products.' This is a new mandatory criterion with no IHR 2005 equivalent.
notes: A WHO-facing obligation but with indirect domestic relevance: recommendations issued by WHO will now be calibrated to global product availability and accessibility. States Parties whose recommendations lead to product scarcity or inequitable access may have grounds to raise concerns during recommendation proceedings. No new domestic anchoring required; however, domestic procurement and stockpiling strategies should factor in the implications of this new criterion.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO recommendations must consider health/care workers, humanitarian situations, and supply chains
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 18(3): Recommendations shall, as appropriate, take into account: (a) the need to facilitate international travel particularly of health and care workers and persons in life-threatening or humanitarian situations (without prejudice to Art. 23); and (b) the need to maintain international supply chains including for relevant health products and food supplies.
notes: A WHO-facing obligation addressing recognized gaps from the COVID-19 experience. No direct domestic anchoring required. However, States Parties wishing to advocate for health worker travel facilitation or supply chain continuity during a PHEIC have a textual basis in Art. 18(3). Domestic emergency plans should account for these considerations when implementing WHO recommendations.

new_or_modified_concept: core capacities language aligned with Annex 1 in general PoE obligations
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 19(a) to read 'core capacities set forth in Annex 1' (adding 'core'). IHR 2005 read 'capacities set forth in Annex 1.' Minor language alignment with the broader 'core capacities' terminology introduced in WHA77.17.
notes: Terminological alignment; no substantive change to the domestic anchoring requirement. The obligations for PoE remain as in IHR 2005 but are now explicitly characterized as 'core capacities,' with all associated implications of the expanded Annex 1.

new_or_modified_concept: core capacities language and WHO certification guidelines for airports and ports
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 20(1) to add 'core' before 'capacities.' Art. 20(5) (new) requires WHO to 'develop and publish the certification guidelines for airports and ports under this Article' and to 'publish a list of certified airports and ports.' IHR 2005 had no Art. 20(5).
notes: Art. 20(5) is a WHO obligation. However, publication of certification guidelines may create de facto benchmarks against which States Parties' designated airports and ports are assessed. States Parties should monitor WHO certification guidelines once published and review whether their designated airports and ports meet those standards.

new_or_modified_concept: core capacities language in ground crossings designation
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 21(1) and Art. 21(2)(b) to add 'core' before 'capacities.' IHR 2005 read 'capacities provided in Annex 1.'
notes: Terminological alignment; no substantive change. Cross-references expanded Annex 1 which now includes prevention and preparedness capacities.

new_or_modified_concept: conveyance operators health measures extended to embarkation and disembarkation
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 24(1)(a) and (b) to add 'including for application on board as well as during embarkation and disembarkation.' IHR 2005 restricted health measure application primarily to on-board obligations.
notes: Explicit extension of conveyance operator health measure obligations to embarkation and disembarkation phases. This may require review of domestic airport/port health measure regulatory frameworks to ensure competent authorities can enforce health measures during boarding and alighting, not only while vessels are in port or aircraft are on the tarmac. Cross-references Annex 4 (IHR24-CHG-059) which mirrors this change.

new_or_modified_concept: quarantine added explicitly to affected conveyances measures
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 27(1) to read 'including isolation and quarantine of the conveyances' (adding 'and quarantine'). IHR 2005 provided only for 'isolation of the conveyances.'
notes: Explicit addition of quarantine as a measure applicable to conveyances (previously only isolation was listed). This may require review of domestic public health emergency legislation to ensure competent authorities at PoE have explicit legal authority to impose quarantine on conveyances, in addition to isolation. Quarantine and isolation may carry different legal definitions and procedural requirements in domestic law.

new_or_modified_concept: free pratique paragraph reference corrected
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 28(3) to read 'subject to the previous paragraph 2 of this Article' (replacing 'subject to the previous paragraph'). This is a technical correction for legal clarity.
notes: Technical/editorial correction only. No substantive change to the free pratique obligation. No domestic legal review required.

new_or_modified_concept: health documents may be in digital or non-digital format
change_summary: WHA77.17 adds Arts. 35(2)-(4): Par. 2: 'Health documents under these Regulations may be issued in non-digital format or digital format, subject to the obligations of any State Party regarding the format of such documents deriving from other international agreements.' Par. 3: Health documents must conform to Annexes and their authenticity shall be ascertainable regardless of format. Par. 4: WHO shall develop technical guidance including specifications or standards for issuance and authenticity of health documents in both formats, consistent with Art. 45 (personal data).
notes: Digital health documents are now explicitly authorized. States Parties must implement systems capable of issuing, accepting, and verifying digital health documents. This requires: (a) digital infrastructure for health document issuance and verification; (b) legal framework for digital document authenticity; (c) interoperability with international digital health document systems; (d) data protection compliance per Art. 45. The phrase 'subject to obligations deriving from other international agreements' (e.g., ICAO digital travel documents) requires cross-instrument legal review. This is among the most technically demanding domestic implementation challenges of WHA77.17.

new_or_modified_concept: Maritime Declaration of Health renamed to Maritime Ship Declaration of Health
change_summary: WHA77.17 renames Art. 37 and the document it governs from 'Maritime Declaration of Health' to 'Maritime Ship Declaration of Health.' The article title now reads 'Maritime Ship Declaration of Health.' Annex 8 is correspondingly renamed.
notes: This renaming requires updates to: domestic maritime regulatory frameworks, port authority procedures, official forms and templates, and bilateral or multilateral maritime agreements that reference the prior document name. IMO was specifically notified of this change per WHA77.17 operative paragraph 3(2). Cross-references Annex 8 (IHR24-CHG-061) and Annex 4 (IHR24-CHG-059) which adopt the new name.

new_or_modified_concept: consultation mechanism for additional health measures expanded; confidentiality obligation added
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 43(7) to allow States Parties to request consultation 'either directly, or through the Director-General, who may also facilitate consultations between the States Parties concerned.' Adds: 'Unless otherwise agreed with the State Parties involved in the consultation, information shared during the consultation must be kept confidential.'
notes: A new confidentiality obligation attaches to information shared during Art. 43(7) consultations. This requires domestic legal or administrative mechanisms to treat such information as confidential. The expanded consultation mechanism (including DG facilitation) may increase diplomatic and procedural complexity for States implementing additional health measures. Review of domestic inter-ministerial procedures for Art. 43 consultations recommended.

new_or_modified_concept: title expanded to include financing; preparedness added to collaboration scope
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends the title of Art. 44 to 'Collaboration, assistance and financing.' Art. 44(1)(a) adds 'preparedness for' to the collaboration scope. Art. 44(1)(c) expands to 'including through relevant sources and funding mechanisms to facilitate implementation of their obligations under these Regulations in particular to address the needs of developing countries.'
notes: Financing is now an express element of the collaboration and assistance framework. States Parties must collaborate on mobilizing financial resources specifically addressed to developing country needs. Art. 44(1)(a) adds preparedness collaboration, aligning with the expanded IHR purpose. Domestic budget allocation for IHR implementation and international health financing cooperation should be reviewed.

new_or_modified_concept: WHO collaboration obligations expanded to include relevant health products access
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 44(2)(d): WHO collaboration with States Parties shall include 'the facilitation of access to relevant health products, in accordance with paragraph 8 of Article 13.' Art. 44(2)(c) is also amended to specify 'developing, strengthening and maintaining the core capacities.'
notes: Primarily a WHO obligation but directly relevant to State Party capacity to engage WHO for health product access facilitation. States Parties should be aware of this mechanism when planning emergency procurement and requesting WHO assistance.

new_or_modified_concept: domestic funding obligation and sustainable financing for IHR implementation
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 44(2 bis): 'States Parties, subject to applicable law and available resources, shall maintain or increase domestic funding, as necessary, and collaborate, including through international cooperation and assistance, as appropriate, to strengthen sustainable financing to support the implementation of these Regulations.'
notes: Art. 44(2 bis) creates an explicit domestic funding obligation: States Parties must maintain or increase domestic funding for IHR implementation. The qualifiers 'subject to applicable law and available resources' provide flexibility but do not remove the obligation. This may require domestic budget process review to ensure dedicated IHR implementation funding. For Mexico, this implies a formal budget line or equivalent allocation for IHR-related activities across relevant ministries.

new_or_modified_concept: inter-State coordination on financing entities and Coordinating Financial Mechanism
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 44(2 ter): States Parties shall collaborate to (a) encourage governance of existing financing entities to be regionally representative and responsive to developing country needs; (b) identify and enable access to financial resources including through the Coordinating Financial Mechanism. Art. 44(2 quater): DG shall support this collaboration work and report outcomes to Health Assembly.
notes: Art. 44(2 ter) creates inter-State coordination obligations specifically aimed at making financing equitable and accessible. The reference to the Coordinating Financial Mechanism (Art. 44 bis) is a forward cross-reference. States Parties must engage with CFM governance processes and ensure their positions reflect developing country needs. Cross-references Art. 54 bis (States Parties Committee for Implementation).

new_or_modified_concept: Coordinating Financial Mechanism established
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 44 bis in its entirety, establishing a Coordinating Financial Mechanism (CFM) to: (a) promote timely, predictable, and sustainable financing for core capacity development, strengthening, and maintenance including for pandemic emergencies; (b) maximize financing availability for developing countries; (c) mobilize new and additional financial resources and increase efficient use of existing instruments. The CFM shall function under Health Assembly authority.
notes: The CFM is an entirely new institutional mechanism with no IHR 2005 counterpart. States Parties must: (a) engage with CFM governance through the States Parties Committee for Implementation (Art. 54 bis); (b) identify domestic capacity gaps eligible for CFM financing; (c) integrate CFM applications into national IHR action plans. The CFM's terms of reference and operationalization modalities are to be adopted at the first States Parties Committee meeting. Legal review required to identify domestic processes for accessing and reporting on CFM funding.

new_or_modified_concept: processing order clarified
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 45(2) to read 'States Parties may process and disclose' (replacing 'disclose and process'). This reordering places processing before disclosure, reflecting correct data protection sequence.
notes: The reordering is normatively significant: processing (including collection, use, storage, analysis) now explicitly precedes and conditions disclosure. This aligns with international data protection principles and implies that personal data must be lawfully processed before it can be disclosed. Domestic data protection frameworks implementing Art. 45 should reflect this sequence. Legal review recommended where existing health data laws permit disclosure without full processing safeguards.

new_or_modified_concept: Emergency Committee scope includes pandemic emergency
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 48(1)(a) and (b) to include 'pandemic emergency' in the Emergency Committee's mandate: providing views on whether an event constitutes a PHEIC including a pandemic emergency, and on the termination thereof.
notes: Aligns the Emergency Committee's mandate with the two-step determination procedure in Art. 12(4 bis). No domestic anchoring required. States Parties should ensure their experts nominated to the Emergency Committee are briefed on the pandemic emergency definition and its additional criteria.

new_or_modified_concept: Emergency Committee subject to WHO Advisory Panel Regulations
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 48(1 bis): 'The Emergency Committee shall be considered an Expert Committee expert committee and shall be subject to the WHO Advisory Panel Regulations, unless otherwise provided for in this Article.' [Note: text appears to contain a drafting artifact with 'Expert Committee expert committee.']
notes: A WHO governance change. No domestic anchoring required. However, the 'unless otherwise provided' clause preserves IHR-specific procedures. Note: the source text contains a possible drafting artifact ('Expert Committee expert committee') which may be corrected in the authentic consolidated text; uncertainty flagged.

new_or_modified_concept: Emergency Committee membership: expert from affected State Party strengthened
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 48(2) from 'At least one member of the Emergency Committee should be an expert nominated by a State(s) Party(ies) within whose territory the event arises' to 'Members of the Emergency Committee should include at least one be an expert nominated by a State(s) Party(ies) within whose territory the event is occurring.' [Note: possible drafting artifact in source.] The change to plural 'Members' and 'is occurring' aligns with multi-State event recognition.
notes: The change from 'a State' to 'State(s) Party(ies)' is consistent with the multi-State recognition in Art. 12. No domestic anchoring required but relevant for States whose territory is involved in a PHEIC, as they have a clearer right to nominate an Emergency Committee expert. Uncertainty flagged regarding 'be an expert' phrasing which may be a drafting artifact.

new_or_modified_concept: DG communications include pandemic emergency; State Party termination proposals
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 49(6) to require DG to communicate the determination and termination of 'a public health emergency of international concern, including a pandemic emergency' to all States Parties. Art. 49(7) (new) confirms that States Parties where the event occurred may propose termination of a PHEIC including pandemic emergency and present to Emergency Committee.
notes: Art. 49(7) formalizes the right of affected States Parties to propose PHEIC/pandemic emergency termination. This is significant for States seeking to lift travel and trade restrictions. No new domestic anchoring required but States should establish internal protocols for preparing and submitting termination proposals to the DG and Emergency Committee.

new_or_modified_concept: IHR functioning review must include financing; WHO must review Annex 2 within one year
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Art. 54(2) to require the Health Assembly to periodically review 'the functioning of these Regulations, including financing for their effective implementation.' Art. 54(3) (new) requires WHO to periodically conduct studies to review and evaluate the functioning of Annex 2; the first such review must commence no later than one year after entry into force.
notes: Art. 54(2) adds financing to the scope of Health Assembly review. This may generate expectations on States Parties to report on domestic IHR financing. Art. 54(3) Annex 2 review is a WHO obligation but the outcomes may require domestic adjustments to notification procedures if Annex 2 is modified. No direct domestic anchoring required at this stage.

new_or_modified_concept: States Parties Committee for Implementation established
change_summary: WHA77.17 introduces Art. 54 bis in its entirety, establishing the States Parties Committee for Implementation (SPCI) to facilitate effective implementation of these Regulations, in particular Arts. 44 and 44 bis. The Committee is facilitativenon-adversarial, non-punitive, assistive, and transparent. It shall: (a) promote learning and exchange of best practices; (b) establish a Subcommittee for technical advice. Comprised of all States Parties, meets at least every two years. The first meeting adopts terms of reference for the CFM.
notes: The SPCI is an entirely new institutional mechanism. States Parties must: (a) designate representatives to participate in SPCI meetings; (b) prepare positions for the first meeting where CFM terms of reference are adopted by consensus; (c) engage with the Subcommittee's technical review process. The SPCI's facilitative and non-punitive character limits its enforcement role but its outputs (best practices, peer learning) may create reputational incentives for compliance. Legal review recommended to identify domestic entities responsible for SPCI engagement and reporting.

new_or_modified_concept: Annex 1 title and section heading expanded to include prevention and preparedness
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends the title of Annex 1 Part A to 'Core Capacities Requirements for Prevention, Surveillance, Preparedness and Response' and Par. 1(a) to list 'prevention, surveillance, reporting, notification, verification, preparedness, response and collaboration activities.' IHR 2005 read 'Core Capacity Requirements for Surveillance and Response.'
notes: Prevention and preparedness are now express elements of the Annex 1 core capacity framework. This expands the scope of domestic obligations beyond detection and response. Domestic public health legislation must be assessed to determine whether prevention and preparedness are adequately mandated and funded as IHR core capacities. Cross-references Art. 5(1) and Art. 13(1) expansions.

new_or_modified_concept: Annex 1 Par. 2 cross-references updated; new State Party collaboration obligation within Annex
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Annex 1 Par. 2 to add reference to Art. 19(a) in addition to Art. 5(1) and Art. 13(1). A new Par. 4 is introduced: 'Pursuant to Article 44, States Parties shall undertake to collaborate with each other, to the extent possible, in developing, strengthening and maintaining core capacities.'
notes: The new Par. 4 embeds the Art. 44 collaboration obligation within Annex 1, reinforcing that core capacity development is a collaborative as well as individual State obligation. This may strengthen the basis for requesting international assistance for core capacity development. No new standalone domestic anchoring required beyond the Art. 44 framework.

new_or_modified_concept: local level core capacities expanded: health services access and community engagement
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Annex 1 Par. 4 (renumbered as '4 1') to add two new local-level core capacities: (d) to prepare for and facilitate access to health services necessary for responding to public health risks and events; and (e) to engage relevant stakeholders, including communities, in preparing for and responding to public health risks and events.
notes: Two new local-level capacities significantly expand community-level obligations. Capacity (d) requires active facilitation of health service access at the local level, not merely detection and reporting. Capacity (e) introduces community and stakeholder engagement as a core capacity obligation. These obligations have strong federalism implications in Mexico where municipal and state health systems operate semi-independently. Legal and institutional review required to determine how local health authorities are empowered to fulfill these new capacities.

new_or_modified_concept: intermediate level capacities expanded: coordination, risk communication, misinformation/disinformation
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Annex 1 Par. 5 (renumbered '5 2') to add a new sub-capacity (c) at the intermediate level: 'to coordinate with and support the Local level in preventing, preparing for and responding to public health risks and events, including in relation to: (i) surveillance; (ii) on-site investigations; (iii) laboratory diagnostics, including referral of samples; (iv) implementation of control measures; (v) access to health services and health products needed for the response; (vi) risk communication, including addressing misinformation and disinformation; and (vii) logistical assistance.' A footnote clarifies that where no intermediate level exists, capacities shall be met at Local or National level.
notes: This is a major expansion of intermediate-level obligations. Most significant is sub-capacity (vi): risk communication including addressing misinformation and disinformation is now a core IHR capacity at the intermediate level. This implies domestic legal authority and operational capacity to address misinformation as part of public health emergency response. Risk communication and misinformation/disinformation frameworks require dedicated institutional capacity and potentially legal mechanisms. The footnote on States without an intermediate level is significant for highly centralized or federal systems.

new_or_modified_concept: national level capacities expanded: surveillance, clinical guidance, health product access, risk communication
change_summary: WHA77.17 substantially amends Annex 1 Par. 6 (renumbered '6 3') national level public health prevention, preparedness and response capacities. New capacities added: (b) surveillance; (g) developing and/or disseminating guidance for clinical case management and infection prevention and control; (h) access to health services and health products needed for the response; (i) risk communication, including addressing misinformation and disinformation; (m bis) coordinating activities nationally and supporting Local and Intermediate levels. Existing capacities reorganized and lettered.
notes: Risk communication and addressing misinformation/disinformation now appear as an explicit national-level core IHR capacity (sub-capacity i), not merely a programmatic activity. This creates a legal basis for national authorities to develop and implement anti-disinformation strategies as part of IHR implementation. Sub-capacity (h) on health product access links to Arts. 13(8)-(9) equitable access framework. Sub-capacity (g) on clinical guidance requires institutional capacity for rapid clinical protocol development. These additions have significant domestic legal anchoring implications for Mexico's CENAPRECE and IMSS/ISSSTE.

new_or_modified_concept: PoE PHEIC response capacity: laboratories added to affected traveller care arrangements
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Annex 1 Part B Par. 2(b) to read: 'to provide assessment of and care for affected travellers or animals by establishing arrangements with local medical and veterinary facilities and laboratories, for their isolation, and treatment, the analysis of their samples, and other support services that may be required.' The phrase 'and laboratories' and 'the analysis of their samples' are new.
notes: Designated PoE must now have formal arrangements with laboratories (not just medical and veterinary facilities) for sample analysis from affected travellers or animals. This requires PoE authorities to identify, contract with, and maintain agreements with laboratories capable of processing samples from potentially infected travellers. In Mexico, this implies coordination between PoE health authorities and reference laboratories (InDRE or equivalent). Legal frameworks for PoE-laboratory arrangements should be reviewed.

new_or_modified_concept: decision instrument middle box expanded: clusters of severe acute respiratory disease
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends the middle box of the Annex 2 decision instrument to add 'in particular clusters of cases of severe acute respiratory disease of unknown or novel cause' as a specific example warranting use of the algorithm. IHR 2005 middle box read only 'any event of potential international public health concern, and those of unknown causes or sources.'
notes: This addition reflects lessons from COVID-19 and SARS. Clusters of severe acute respiratory disease of unknown or novel cause are now explicitly flagged as events requiring Annex 2 algorithm application. This has operational implications for national surveillance: detection systems must specifically flag and escalate such clusters. Domestic surveillance protocols and case definitions should be updated to reflect this explicit notification trigger. WHO is also required to conduct a broader Annex 2 review within one year of entry into force (Art. 54(3)).

new_or_modified_concept: Ship Sanitation Certificate model: reference to renamed declaration
change_summary: The model in Annex 3 (Ship Sanitation Control Exemption Certificate/Ship Sanitation Control Certificate) contains a footnote reference to 'Maritime Ship Declaration of Health' (renamed from 'Maritime Declaration of Health'). The Annex 3 model is otherwise maintained with technical content.
notes: Administrative update to reflect the Art. 37/Annex 8 renaming. Domestic forms and port authority procedures referencing 'Maritime Declaration of Health' should be updated to 'Maritime Ship Declaration of Health.' No substantive change to certification requirements.

new_or_modified_concept: technical requirements for conveyances: health measures during embarkation/disembarkation; certificate renamed
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Annex 4 Section A Par. 1(c) to add 'including on board as well as during embarkation and disembarkation' — mirroring the Art. 24(1) change. Par. 2 references 'Maritime Ship Declaration of Health' (renamed from 'Maritime Declaration of Health').
notes: Mirrors Art. 24(1) change (IHR24-CHG-029). Conveyance operators' technical requirements now explicitly cover health measures during embarkation and disembarkation phases. PoE competent authorities and conveyance operator regulatory frameworks must be updated. Administrative update also required for document references.

new_or_modified_concept: vaccination certificates: digital format authorized; signature requirements for non-digital only; guardian provisions updated
change_summary: WHA77.17 amends Annex 6 to: (a) Par. 4: Require that non-digital certificates be signed in hand by the clinician ('in non-digital format must be signed in the hand of by the clinician'); add that regardless of format, certificates must bear the clinician's name or relevant authority's name. (b) Par. 8: Restrict parent/guardian signature requirements to non-digital format; add that persons with a guardian shall have the guardian sign on their behalf. (c) Model certificate: Add clinician name or relevant authority name field regardless of format; clarify format-specific elements.
notes: The introduction of digital vaccination certificates requires: (a) a domestic digital certificate issuance system; (b) legal recognition of digital vaccination certificates as equivalent to non-digital; (c) authenticity verification mechanisms; (d) data protection compliance per Art. 35(4)/Art. 45. The updated guardian provision is both a legal clarification and a rights safeguard. States must review domestic vaccination certificate issuance procedures for both digital and non-digital pathways. For Mexico, this requires coordination between COFEPRIS, SSa, and health digital infrastructure managers.

new_or_modified_concept: Maritime Declaration of Health renamed to Maritime Ship Declaration of Health
change_summary: WHA77.17 renames Annex 8 from 'Maritime Declaration of Health' to 'Maritime Ship Declaration of Health.' The model form content is maintained with corresponding title update.
notes: Renaming requires updates to all domestic maritime regulatory instruments, port authority procedures, and official forms that reference the prior name. IMO was specifically notified (WHA77.17 operative par. 3(2)). Cross-references Art. 37 (IHR24-CHG-033) and Annex 4 (IHR24-CHG-055). Administrative update; no substantive change to declaration content requirements.
